section.title: Out of the Depths I Cry
section.sectionRationale: A Song of Ascents, eleventh in the pilgrim collection Psalms 120 to 134, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. Traditionally sung as pilgrims neared Jerusalem with cleared consciences, this psalm moves from the depths of guilt to the height of confident, waiting hope in the LORD's abundant redemption.
section.thesis: Out of the depths of sin and guilt, the psalmist cries to the LORD, finding not judgment but forgiveness, and waits for Him with a hope grounded in His steadfast love and plentiful redemption from all iniquity, a redemption fully accomplished in Christ.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the cry from the depths, verses 1 to 2, the confession of universal guilt and the hope of forgiveness, verses 3 to 4, patient waiting for the LORD, verses 5 to 6, and the closing call for Israel to hope in the LORD's abundant redemption, verses 7 to 8.

units.label: Out of the depths I cry to You

verses.text: Out of the depths I cry to You, O LORD!
O Lord, hear my voice; let Your ears be attentive to my plea for mercy.
If You, O LORD, kept track of iniquities, then who, O Lord, could stand?
But with You there is {{selichah|forgiveness}}, so that You may be feared.

units.label: My soul waits for the LORD

verses.text: I wait for the LORD; my soul does wait, and in His word I put my hope.
My soul waits for the Lord more than watchmen wait for the morning, more than watchmen wait for the morning.

units.label: He will redeem Israel from all iniquity

verses.text: O Israel, put your hope in the LORD, for with the LORD is loving devotion, and with Him is {{padah|redemption}} in abundance.
And He will redeem Israel from all iniquity.

selichah.gloss: Forgiveness, pardon; a rare and warm term for God's willingness to wipe away guilt, given here as the reason His people fear and revere Him, not flee from Him, verse 4.

padah.gloss: To redeem, ransom, buy back by paying a price; the LORD's redemption of Israel from all iniquity is described as abundant and certain, verses 7 to 8.

basic.context: This Song of Ascents gives voice to a soul sunk in the depths of guilt, crying out for mercy, confessing that no one could stand if the LORD marked iniquities, and finding hope not in self-effort but in the LORD's forgiveness and abundant, promised redemption.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes one believer's honest cry from guilt to hope, and it prescribes as an abiding pattern honest confession of sin, patient waiting on the LORD's word, and confident hope in His forgiveness and full redemption, for the individual and for the whole people of God.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm moves from maamaqqim, depths, an image of overwhelming, drowning distress, to selichah, forgiveness, in verse 4, and finally to padah, redemption by payment of a price, in verses 7 to 8, tracing guilt's descent and grace's ascent in three key words.
basic.typology: The cry from the depths anticipates every sinner's cry for mercy answered in Christ, and the promise that the LORD will redeem Israel from all iniquity anticipates the full and final redemption accomplished at the cross.
basic.christ: The forgiveness this psalm marvels at, and the abundant redemption it promises, are accomplished fully in Christ, in whom believers have redemption through His blood, the forgiveness of trespasses, Ephesians 1:7, and who came to save His people from their sins, Matthew 1:21.
basic.god: The LORD hears the cry of the depths and is attentive to pleas for mercy, He does not deal with His people according to their iniquities but forgives, and He is the source of steadfast love and abundant, certain redemption from all sin.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 1:7 declares redemption through Christ's blood, the forgiveness of trespasses, the fulfillment of this psalm's hope. Titus 2:14 shows Christ redeeming a people for Himself from all lawlessness, echoing 'redeem Israel from all iniquity.' Matthew 1:21 names Jesus as the one who will save His people from their sins.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's cry from the depths, its wonder at forgiveness, and its hope in abundant redemption are all fulfilled in Christ, who bore the full weight of sin so that His people might be forgiven and redeemed from all their iniquities.

context.literary: A Song of Ascents structured as a movement from despair to hope, an urgent cry, verses 1 to 2, a theological confession, verses 3 to 4, patient waiting, verses 5 to 6, and a communal exhortation to hope, verses 7 to 8.
context.historical: Its placement near the end of the Ascents collection suits pilgrims approaching Jerusalem having examined their consciences, and Jewish tradition also links it to seasons of exile and longed-for national restoration.
context.canonical: Follows Psalm 129's honest reckoning with affliction from without with an equally honest reckoning with guilt from within, and anticipates the New Testament's full unveiling of forgiveness and redemption accomplished in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach God's people to cry honestly to the LORD from the depths of guilt, to marvel at His forgiveness rather than presume upon it, and to wait patiently and hopefully for His promised, abundant redemption from all iniquity.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes one believer's personal cry and confession, widening to address the whole nation. It prescribes as an abiding pattern honest confession of sin, patient hope grounded in God's word, and confidence in His abundant forgiveness and redemption for all who cry to Him.

debates.question: Is the 'depths' of verse 1 primarily the guilt of sin, or a broader distress such as suffering or exile?

views.label: Primarily the depths of personal guilt
views.case: Verses 3 to 4 move immediately to iniquities and forgiveness, suggesting the depths are chiefly the psalmist's own sin and its weight.

views.label: A broader distress that includes but is not limited to guilt
views.case: The Hebrew maamaqqim elsewhere pictures overwhelming waters of any kind of crisis, and the psalm's national widening in verses 7 to 8 suggests exile or corporate trouble is also in view.

debates.bearing: Either reading leads to the same hope, that whatever depths a person or nation faces, guilt included, the LORD's forgiveness and abundant redemption meet it fully.

study.originalLanguages: The psalm traces guilt's descent and grace's ascent through three key words, maamaqqim, the depths of overwhelming distress, verse 1, selichah, forgiveness, a rare and warm term for God wiping away guilt, verse 4, and padah, redemption by payment of a price, verses 7 to 8.

typology.type: The cry from the depths for mercy, verses 1 to 2
typology.fulfillment: Every sinner's cry for mercy answered fully in Christ, who bore sin's weight at the cross, Ephesians 1:7.

typology.type: Abundant redemption from all iniquity, verse 8
typology.fulfillment: Christ's redeeming death, purchasing a people for Himself and cleansed from all lawlessness, Titus 2:14.

study.christ: The forgiveness this psalm marvels at, and the abundant redemption it promises for Israel, are accomplished fully in Christ. In Him, believers have redemption through His blood, the forgiveness of trespasses, according to the riches of His grace, Ephesians 1:7. He came, as His very name declares, to save His people from their sins, Matthew 1:21, and gave Himself to redeem a people for His own possession, purified from all lawlessness, Titus 2:14, the full and final answer to this psalm's hope that the LORD would redeem Israel from all iniquity.
study.god: The LORD hears the cry of the depths and is attentive to pleas for mercy, He does not deal with His people according to their iniquities but forgives, and He is the source of steadfast love and abundant, certain redemption from all sin, both for the individual soul and for His whole people.

crossReferences.note: Redemption through Christ's blood, the forgiveness of trespasses, the fulfillment of this psalm's hope of forgiveness.
Christ redeems a people for Himself from all lawlessness, echoing the promise to redeem Israel from all iniquity.
Jesus is named as the one who will save His people from their sins, the person in whom this psalm's hope is fulfilled.

study.oneStory: This psalm's cry from the depths, its wonder at forgiveness, and its hope in abundant redemption are all fulfilled in Christ, who bore the full weight of sin at the cross so that His people might be forgiven and redeemed from all their iniquities, both now and forever.

sermon.title: An Exposition of Psalm 130, Part One
sermon.why: Begg expounds Psalm 130 directly, encouraging listeners to follow the psalmist's example by crying out to Jesus for mercy in the depths of sin's despair and trusting Christ's sacrifice as the cure for iniquity.

quiz.q: Where does the psalmist cry from, according to verse 1?

options.text: Out of the depths, to the LORD.
options.note: This is the exact opening cry and image of the psalm.

options.text: From a place of comfortable ease.
options.note: The psalm describes overwhelming distress, the opposite of comfortable ease.

options.text: From the top of a mountain in triumph.
options.note: The image is descent into depths, not ascent to a triumphant height.

options.text: From a courtroom defending his innocence.
options.note: The psalmist does not claim innocence; he cries for mercy from a place of need.

quiz.q: According to verse 3, what would happen if the LORD kept track of iniquities?

options.text: No one could stand before Him.
options.note: This is the psalm's exact confession of universal guilt before a holy God.

options.text: Only the wicked would be affected, not the righteous.
options.note: The verse implies universal inability to stand, not a distinction sparing the righteous.

options.text: God would simply overlook minor sins.
options.note: The verse presents a serious reckoning, not casual overlooking of sin.

options.text: Everyone would stand except the psalmist himself.
options.note: The rhetorical question implies universal inability to stand, including the psalmist.

quiz.q: What does verse 4 say is the reason the LORD is to be feared?

options.text: Because with Him there is forgiveness.
options.note: This is the psalm's striking claim, that forgiveness, not mere terror, produces true reverence.

options.text: Because He punishes without mercy.
options.note: The verse ties fear of the LORD directly to His forgiveness, not merciless punishment.

options.text: Because He is distant and uninvolved.
options.note: The psalm portrays an attentive, engaged God, not a distant one.

options.text: Because His forgiveness is rare and unreliable.
options.note: The psalm portrays forgiveness as a settled, reliable characteristic of the LORD, not something rare or uncertain.

quiz.q: How does verse 6 describe the intensity of the psalmist's waiting for the LORD?

options.text: More than watchmen wait for the morning.
options.note: This vivid, doubled comparison is the exact image the psalm uses for intense, expectant waiting.

options.text: Less eagerly than a child waits for a meal.
options.note: The psalm describes waiting that exceeds even watchmen's intense longing for dawn, not a lesser degree of eagerness.

options.text: With complete indifference to the outcome.
options.note: The psalm's waiting is intensely expectant, not indifferent.

options.text: Only during the daytime hours.
options.note: The watchmen imagery specifically evokes nighttime vigilance awaiting dawn, not daytime waiting.

quiz.q: What does the psalm promise in verses 7 to 8 regarding Israel's redemption?

options.text: With the LORD is abundant redemption, and He will redeem Israel from all iniquity.
options.note: This is the psalm's climactic promise of full, abundant redemption fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: Redemption is available only for a select few sins.
options.note: The psalm promises redemption from all iniquity, not a limited selection of sins.

options.text: Israel must redeem herself through good works before God will act.
options.note: The redemption described flows from the LORD's initiative and loving devotion, not self-achieved merit.

options.text: Redemption will come only after many more centuries of waiting.
options.note: The psalm expresses confident, near expectation, not an indefinite postponement of hope.

questions: The psalmist cries 'out of the depths' rather than pretending everything is fine. What depths of guilt, grief, or despair do you need to bring honestly to God right now?
The psalm confesses that no one could stand if the LORD marked iniquities, yet finds hope in His forgiveness. How does resting in Christ's forgiveness change the way you deal with your own failures?
The psalmist waits for the LORD 'more than watchmen wait for the morning.' What are you currently waiting on God for, and how intensely are you actually watching and hoping for His answer?
The psalm promises abundant redemption from all iniquity, not merely partial forgiveness. Is there a particular sin or shame you struggle to believe is truly, fully forgiven in Christ?
This individual's cry widens into a call for all Israel to hope in the LORD. Who in your life needs you to share this same hope of forgiveness and redemption found in Christ?

translationNotes.note: Depths, maamaqqim, elsewhere pictures overwhelming, chaotic waters, as in Jonah 2:3, painting guilt and distress as a kind of drowning.
Forgiveness, selichah, is a comparatively rare Hebrew word, used only a handful of times in the Old Testament, underlining how remarkable and deliberate God's pardon is.
Redemption, padah, pictures a ransom paid to buy back something or someone from bondage, the same root behind the redemption of the firstborn in Exodus 13:13.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 130 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 124-132). Verse count (8) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 130:1-8. One em dash in verse 6 was converted to a comma; no other em or en dashes present. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's sermon 'An Exposition of Psalm 130, Part One' (on Psalm 130:1-3) at truthforlife.org.
